Based on the evidence gathered about the pes anserinus involving multiple muscle tendons inserting onto the same area of the tibia, we see similarities and differences across the options presented:
Similarities exist in how certain muscles converge at points elsewhere like in the neck and shoulder girdles too - yet those don't involve three separate tendons coming together quite similarly. TMJ does have concurrent muscle attachments around temporomandibular joint but lacks pes anserine's distinctive triad confluence pattern. Parotid gland and submandibular gland are salivary organs without any analogous muscular attachments.

There seems to be significant structural resemblance between the Pes Anserinus and the temporal mandibular joint (TMJ) given the convergence of multiple muscle tendons in both locations. While the pes anserinus has a very specific triangular confluence of three tendons—the sartorius, gracilis, and semitendinosus—at a single point on the tibia, the TMJ also exhibits a similar principle of multiple muscle groups converging nearby. However, none of the other options present (parotid, submandibular gland, cheek) demonstrate this specific arrangement of tendons from different muscles joining forces at one location. Given this evidence and analysis, I reaffirm my agreement with Option A:
